text:
*[Bug 19071] wilcox.test and precision when detecting zeros*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19071>

--- Comment #8 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
(In reply to Martin Maechler from comment #5)
As a matter of fact, scale-equivariance is not given for really large numbers
with the `min.d = 0` default. I have updated the ?zapsmall help page now, soon
to be visible at

<https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-devel/packages/base/refman/base.html#zapsmall>
to say so.

Also from my experiments, the R regression tests, and some quiet thinking,
I've now seen that the __robustness__ of `wilcox.test()` to outliers is indeed
more important than full scale equivariance, and we actually not only need
`zapsmall()` but actually `zapsmall(*, digits, mFUN = <robust>)`.  My current
approach and not-yet-commited R code now using a new (`{stats}` namespace
hidden)

```
.zapsmall.r <- function(x, digits)
    zapsmall(x, digits,
             mFUN = function(x, ina) {
                 x <- abs(x[is.finite(x)])
                 if(length(x <- x[x > 0])) quantile(x, .75, names=FALSE) else 0
             },
             min.d = -324)
```
(the non-default `min.d` value providing scale-equivariance also for large
"scale" numbers).

*[Bug 17549] When building a package, use inRbuildignore() before copying package contents to Tdir*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=17549>

Ben Bolker (<mailto:bbolker@gmail.com|bbolker@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |bbolker@gmail.com

--- Comment #4 from Ben Bolker (<mailto:bbolker@gmail.com|bbolker@gmail.com>) ---
I'll just point out that this continues to be an issue.  There are plenty of
workarounds, but it will probably continue to bite people.  (Maybe one tiny
mitigation would be to issue a "recursively copying package directory ..."
message to hint to users what might be happening?)

Thread from 2024:
<https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2024-August/083610.html>

Thread from 2026: <https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2026-May/084571.html>

(is `rsync` in the list of tools that are expected to be available for R
package building ... ??)  I don't think so, as you don't need `Rtools` for a
source-only package ... ?

text:
*[Bug 15189] Copying a Graphics Window Copies All Graphic Elements Rather Than Bitmap*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=15189>

--- Comment #2 from Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) ---
If I understand correctly, this is because the copy handler for images (in
qdCocoa.m) currently builds a PDF from the graphics device snapshot, and that
PDF uses individual entities for e.g. points and so would potentially be large
as suggested.

A potential solution here would be to include a bitmap representation of the
plot as well, but there are open questions around things like:

- Do we want to render both versions for the Pasteboard? Or just one?
- If just one, do we want to allow the user to configure this behavior?
- Do nothing; this is an older bug report and presumably most people are
comfortable with the current behavior, or just write plots to file with
explicit graphics device invocations where needed.

A quick and dirty patch proposal is included, but is intended to sit only as a
jumping-off point, not a ready-to-merge patch.

If I understand correctly, this is because the copy handler for images (in
qdCocoa.m) currently builds a PDF from the graphics device snapshot, and that
PDF uses individual entities for e.g. points and so would potentially be large
as suggested.

A potential solution here would be to include a bitmap representation of the
plot as well, but there are open questions around things like:

- Do we want to render both versions for the Pasteboard? Or just one?
- If just one, do we want to allow the user to configure this behavior?
- Do nothing; this is an older bug report and presumably most people are
comfortable with the current behavior, or just write plots to file with
explicit graphics device invocations where needed.

A quick and dirty patch proposal is included, but is intended to sit only as a
jumping-off point, not a ready-to-merge patch.

text:
*[Bug 16615] Allow multi-character separator in scan()*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=16615>

Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |TRIAGED
                 CC|                            |kevinushey@gmail.com
     Ever confirmed|0                           |1

--- Comment #1 from Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) ---
Making this change is harder than it looks, unfortunately, and so I think this
is unlikely to happen. I suspect the simplest change here would be to recommend
an alternate tool for when one wants to read data using multi-byte separators,
or to perform some amount of preprocessing and perhaps post-processing to
transform separators into a single byte.

Some more analysis below.

---

  The relevant code is src/main/scan.c. The inner reader (fillBuffer, lines
  ~397-456) consumes one byte at a time and tests it against a single int
  sepchar, with exactly one byte of pushback (LocalData.save, unscanchar at
  line 231). Allowing a multi-byte separator changes several things at once:

  1. Look-ahead and rollback. On seeing the first byte of sep, we have to
     read up to N-1 more bytes and either commit (it was the separator) or
     back out, copying the consumed prefix into the field. The existing
     1-byte pushback isn't enough.

  2. CSV-style quoting. The current code peeks one byte after a closing
     quote to decide "is this the separator or end-of-line?" That peek
     becomes a multi-byte compare with the same rollback issue.

  3. DBCS locales. The reader is byte-oriented but treats DBCS lead/trail
     bytes specially. A multi-byte separator must not be allowed to straddle
     a character boundary, which adds another validity check.

  4. strip.white interactions. A separator like " | " or "\t|\t" makes the
     trim rules ambiguous and needs documented precedence.

  5. Embedded newlines. This is the bigger issue for the use case you
     describe. scan() unconditionally treats \n, \r, and EOF as record
     terminators (see lines 398-399). Even with a multi-byte field
     separator, an embedded newline still breaks the record. So multi-byte
     sep alone wouldn't actually solve the "arbitrary character content"
     problem you mention -- it solves the collision problem, but not the
     embedded-newline problem.

  None of this is unfixable, but it's a delicate change in a hot,
  locale-sensitive path, with a wide test surface across scan/read.table/
  read.csv. The bar to land it is high.


  Alternatives
  ------------

  For the "delimiter unlikely to collide with data" case, the canonical
  solution is a single-byte ASCII control character. C0 0x1E (Record
  Separator) and 0x1F (Unit Separator) were reserved by ASCII for exactly
  this purpose and never appear in well-formed text:

      sep = "\x1f"   # Unit Separator: between fields
                     # \x1e Record Separator: between records

  This works with current scan() and is strictly stronger than a long
  sentinel string, because the byte is by construction absent from text
  payloads.

  If the data already arrives with a multi-byte separator and you can't
  change the producer, substitute before parsing:

      raw <- readChar(path, <http://file.info|file.info>(path)$size, useBytes = TRUE)
      raw <- gsub("|%$!?MYSEP?!$%|", "\x1f", raw,
                  fixed = TRUE, useBytes = TRUE)

  Reading the whole file in one shot (rather than line-at-a-time) avoids
  the issue that readLines()-based approaches have with embedded newlines.

  If your fields can also contain literal \n characters that should not
  terminate a record, you'll need to substitute those too (e.g. to \x1e)
  before handing to scan().

                 CC|                            |kevinushey@gmail.com
     Ever confirmed|0                           |1

text:
*[Bug 18035] a bug in wilcox.test() caused by precision error when there are ties*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18035>

--- Comment #7 from Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) ---
Aidan Lakshman shared a nice relevant blog post:

<https://aakinshin.net/posts/mw-confusing-tie-correction/>

My takeaway is it's very likely a bad choice to use `digits.rank=Inf` since it
will almost surely give misleading `p`-values, but that there's also nothing
like universal agreement about how to fix this, so giving the user full control
may be the right compromise.

I was also trying to get Gemini to be my research assistant here to come up
with a well-motivated default, and it wrote a very nice-looking report, but I'm
afraid I don't have the right combination of (1) confidence that it's doing the
right thing and (2) time to thoroughly vet the claims; to actually pass it
along.

FWIW, [SciPy handles this with no rounding and a documentation
warning](<https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.wilcoxon.html>):

> When y is provided, wilcoxon calculates the test statistic based on the ranks of the absolute values of d = x - y. Roundoff error in the subtraction can result in elements of d being assigned different ranks even when they would be tied with exact arithmetic. Rather than passing x and y separately, consider computing the difference x - y, rounding as needed to ensure that only truly unique elements are numerically distinct, and passing the result as x, leaving y at the default (None).

) ---
Aidan Lakshman shared a nice relevant blog post:
My takeaway is it's very likely a bad choice to use

since it
will almost surely give misleading

-values, but that there's also nothing
like universal agreement about how to fix this, so giving the user full control
may be the right compromise.

I was also trying to get Gemini to be my research assistant here to come up
with a well-motivated default, and it wrote a very nice-looking report, but I'm
afraid I don't have the right combination of (1) confidence that it's doing the
right thing and (2) time to thoroughly vet the claims; to actually pass it
along.

FWIW, [SciPy handles this with no rounding and a documentation
warning](

When y is provided, wilcoxon calculates the test statistic based on the ranks of the absolute values of d = x - y. Roundoff error in the subtraction can result in elements of d being assigned different ranks even when they would be tied with exact arithmetic. Rather than passing x and y separately, consider computing the difference x - y, rounding as needed to ensure that only truly unique elements are numerically distinct, and passing the result as x, leaving y at the default (None).
